text: two thinkers were in fact occupied with two very different and incommensurable projects. Descartes’ main focus was not politics, and he proposed no theory of the state to compete with the Hobbesian construct. Hobbes, on the contrary, was highly involved in science, and was, just like Descartes, engaged in developing a general philosophy. However, we read Hobbes today almost exclusively because of his political ideas. Even if contemporaries considered Hobbes a scientist (Malcolm 2002: 51) his interest in geometry and optics is included in the literature on Hobbes mostly to the extent that it is regarded as having shaped his view of politics (for an exception, see Pacchi 1965). We treat the two thinkers as forefathers within two different realms: Hobbes is the founder of modern politics, while Descartes is the founder of modern science and philosophy. Put simply therefore, Hobbes and Descartes are rarely read together because they posited almost opposite philosophical anthropologies as the building stones of their thought, and because their works took off (or rather, were carried off) in different directions. The important connections between Hobbes and Descartes were taken up most explicitly by Richard Tuck (2002 [1988] and 1998). Tuck discussed how the two thinkers were drawn into the same academic landscape via the group of philosophers gathering around Mersenne, and indicated the extent to which Hobbes may have developed his philosophy through his reading of Descartes. Tuck also stressed how their projects resembled each other: they both wanted to establish a new philosophy, and they agreed that whereas human perception of the world was irremediably faulty, a new departure must be made by regarding sense-impressions as the object (rather than the basis) of investigation. Indeed, beyond their disagreements (which were indeed substantial), Hobbes and Descartes may very consciously have considered each other rivals in laying claim to this idea (Tuck 2002: 28–31). The idea was revolutionary because while accepting the scepticism of antiquity, it offered an alternative: a modern philosophy and a new scientific world image departing from the self. Hobbes and Descartes were not ‘too different’, but all too similar. Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 115 The argument we wish to propose here does not build on the actual correspondence between Hobbes and Descartes as this was mediated by their mutual friend, Marin Mersenne; nor will the connections between the two thinkers be biographically or textually traced. Rather than focusing on their substantial disputes or disagreements, it will instead be suggested that Hobbes’ and Descartes’ epistemological claims represent complementary ways of instituting modernity. The parallels between Hobbes and Descartes exist at the level of morphology, in the kind of ‘absolute’ boundary they draw around the modern state and the modern self, respectively. Their writings can be compared although the order that they establish is substantially different, and although they principally apply it to two very different kinds of ‘interior’: the sovereign state and the sovereign self. These underlying parallels, and the joint Weltbild they served to conjure, were captured by Borkenau in his landmark work, Der Übergang vom feudalen zum bürgerlichen Weltbild: Studien zur Geschichte der Philosophie der Manufakturperiode (Borkenau 1976 [1936]). Secularity in Modernity One of the landmarks of the modern world-view that developed from the seventeenth century was the introduction of secular principles in both thought and political organization, and Hobbes and Descartes are rightly seen as founders in exactly this sense: in the grounding of their systems of thought they abandoned, once and for all, a divinely-ordained legitimization. In seeking out a new ground, they both discovered a new type of principle which could supersede and replace religion and its dangerous divisive tendencies that had shattered Europe during the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries. In most readings, Hobbes and Descartes are therefore also primary figures in the rationalization of thought and practice that paved the way for a wider secularization process, as a new boundary between the secular and the sacred would slowly become accepted and institutionalized. In this sense, the development of thought in the mid seventeenth century is rightly considered a forerunner to the Enlightenment, and both Hobbes and Descartes must beyond doubt be considered as path-breaking transitional figures toward European modernity. The debate between secular and religious interpretations of Hobbes has been raging since at least 1953, when Hood published The Divine Politics of Thomas Hobbes as a challenge to the more dominant secular interpretations, which tend to see Hobbes’ religious writings as opportunistic and in no way foundational for his real aim: creating a secular political theory built onto a philosophical materialism. However, the debate has been an integral part of the reception of both Descartes’ and Hobbes’ works ever since they were published in the seventeenth century. The exact terms of the debate have changed, but the main questions remain: is religion central to their systems of thought? If so, then how so? Are their religious writings really combinable with their science? Did Hobbes and Descartes really care about God and the Bible, or were they (consciously?) paving the way for the final victory of a secular, scientific world-view? Liminality and the Modern 116 These questions inform not only our understanding of Hobbes and Descartes: they pertain to the broader discussion of the religious foundations of modernity. Yet whilst Hobbes and Descartes certainly were masters of rationalization, neither of them actually introduced a separation between sacred and secular. Rather, they triumphantly introduced a new ‘secularity’ through a reorganization of Christian theology in the worldly realm of politics and thought. The Hobbesian state was secular only at one level, and the same can be argued of the Cartesian self. In what is presented here, the historical realities of specific types of state formation will not be discussed, as the focus is on thought symbolism. Even here the argument should not be seen as a contribution to the vast scholarship that exists on these two authors and their impact, as no new material will be presented.
